coredata.dc:title: A sandwich boronate affinity sorbent assay for glucose detection facilitated by boronic acid-terminated fluorescent polymers
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Chaozhan
Li, Yinli
Wei, Yinmao
coredata.dc:description: Glucose monitoring is very important in clinical diagnosis, control and therapy of diabetes. Fluorescent methods based on boronate affinity recognition have attracted much attention in recent years. However, severe interferences from endogenous fluorophores or quenchers in samples are big concerns. In this work, a boronic acid terminated fluorescent polymer with multiple repeated fluorogens was synthesized by copolymerization of a fluorescent monomer [9-anthracenemethyl acrylate (9-AnMA)] and methyl methacrylate (MMA) via atom transfer radical polymerization (ATRP), and a sandwich boronate affinity sorbent assay (SBASA) for selective and ultrasensitive detection of glucose was developed for the first time based on the distinguished feature of glucose which can form a bidentate glucose–boronic complex, using the fluorescent polymer for signal amplification. The proposed method could avoid the effects from typical interfering substances. Compared with previously reported methods, the SBASA has higher selectivity, wider linear range (0.003 to 3.0mM) and higher sensitivity (a detection limit of 0.8μM). Moreover, the developed method was successfully applied to the detection of glucose in human serum samples. The simple, rapid, convenient, and reliable SBASA provided a new concept and an attractive idea for glucose monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sandwich boronate affinity sorbent assay
Fluorescent polymer
Boronic acid
Glucose detection
Serum

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of phenylboronic acid tagged ATRP initiator (3)
Synthesis of the boronic acid terminated poly (9-anthracenemethyl acrylate-co-methyl methacrylate) [(HO)2B-P(AnMA-co-MMA), 5]
Synthesis of the boronic acid terminated fluorescent polymer
SBASA for glucose detection utilizing boronic acid-terminated fluorescent polymers
Analysis of glucose in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of the SBASA for selective and ultrasensitive detection of serum glucose.

(A) FT-IR spectra of the 9-anthracenemethanol (a), 9-AnMA (b) and (HO)2B-P(AnMA-co-MMA) (c). (B) Excitation (d) and emission (e) spectra of (HO)2B-P(AnMA-co-MMA) in the THF solution. The excitation and emission wavelengths were set at 358nm and 418nm respectively.
Effects of adsorption time of glucose (a), adsorption time of (HO)2B-P(AnMA-co-MMA) (b) and the adsorbent dose (c) on the fluorescence intensities.

(A) Fluorescent response of the SBASA to different concentrations of glucose (0, 0.001, 0.003, 0.01, 0.03, 0.1, 0.3, 1.0, 3.0, 5.0mM), excitation wavelength was set at 358nm. (B) Plot of the fluorescence intensity change (at 418nm) vs. glucose concentration. Error bars represent the standard deviations of replicate measurements with n=3. F0 and F are fluorescence intensities of the polymer probe in the absence and presence of the analytes, respectively.

Selectivity of the SBASA. The concentrations (from left to right) are 280μM, 80μM, 400μM, 80μM, 3×10−3 μM, 16μM, 100μM, 670mM, 0.1μM, 0.1μM, 3mM, 3mM, 3mM, 3mM, 1mM, respectively. F 0 and F are fluorescent intensities of the polymer probe before and after adding the corresponding substances, respectively.
The recoveries of glucose in serum samples (n =3).
Comparisons of the present method and recently reported boronate affinity based fluorescent methods for glucose detection.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: QDs, quantum dots; NM, not mentioned in the reference; NA, not applicable, means that no real sample was used in the reported work, therefore there was no protein in the sample solution.

head.ce:title: A sandwich boronate affinity sorbent assay for glucose detection facilitated by boronic acid-terminated fluorescent polymers

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Synthetic and Natural Functional Molecule Chemistry of Ministry of Education Key Laboratory, Key Laboratory of Modern Separation Science in Shaanxi Province, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Northwest University, Xi’an 710127, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Synthetic and Natural Functional Molecule Chemistry of Ministry of Education Key Laboratory, Key Laboratory of Modern Separation Science in Shaanxi Province, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Northwest University
College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Northwest University, 1 Xue Fu Avenue, Changan District, Xi’an 710127, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Glucose monitoring is very important in clinical diagnosis, control and therapy of diabetes. Fluorescent methods based on boronate affinity recognition have attracted much attention in recent years. However, severe interferences from endogenous fluorophores or quenchers in samples are big concerns. In this work, a boronic acid terminated fluorescent polymer with multiple repeated fluorogens was synthesized by copolymerization of a fluorescent monomer [9-anthracenemethyl acrylate (9-AnMA)] and methyl methacrylate (MMA) via atom transfer radical polymerization (ATRP), and a sandwich boronate affinity sorbent assay (SBASA) for selective and ultrasensitive detection of glucose was developed for the first time based on the distinguished feature of glucose which can form a bidentate glucose–boronic complex, using the fluorescent polymer for signal amplification. The proposed method could avoid the effects from typical interfering substances. Compared with previously reported methods, the SBASA has higher selectivity, wider linear range (0.003 to 3.0mM) and higher sensitivity (a detection limit of 0.8μM). Moreover, the developed method was successfully applied to the detection of glucose in human serum samples. The simple, rapid, convenient, and reliable SBASA provided a new concept and an attractive idea for glucose monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: There is a clear and unmet clinical need for improved glucose monitoring, because diabetes mellitus is one of the biggest public health threats [1]. Sugars are essential biological molecules, besides the need for glucose monitoring in the case of diabetes patients, it is also essential for non-diabetic acute care patients to monitor glucose levels [2]. Consequently there is a high demand for blood glucose monitoring and far various methods have been presented, including nanoparticle aggregation-induced colorimetric detection [3,4], electrochemical [5] and fluorescence detection [6,7]. Among various detection methods, fluorescence detection is more attractive as it provides a couple of advantages including high sensitivity, simplicity of operation and non-invasiveness. Rational design of fluorogenic probes is a key for highly sensitive fluorescence detection [8]. Two strategies are typically employed in fluorescence detection of glucose. One is based on the enzyme and enzymatic hydrolysate, such as employing glucose oxidase [9–11]. The other is based on non-enzymes, such as synthetic receptors which could recognize glucose [12]. Compared with the enzymatic system, the non-enzyme detection system has many merits, such as higher stability, lower cost, and easier manufacturing. Boronic acid has been shown to be a very good receptor for saccharides, which is based on the principle that boronic acids can reversibly bind with cis-1,2- or 1,3-diols via covalent interaction to form five- or six-membered cyclic esters. Generally simple monoboronic acid based detection systems have poor selectivity toward glucose, and other monosaccharides especially fructose can largely affect the determination accuracy [13–15]. Glucose selective boronic acid-based sensors are typically diboronic acids that are able to form 1:1 cyclic boronate esters with glucose, utilizing the unique feature that glucose has two binding sites to boronic acid [13]. However, many of the reported diboronic acid sensors exhibited several disadvantages, including substantial synthetic demand and poor water solubility [13]. A simpler alternative would be to find efficient approaches to improve the inherent selectivity of monoboronic acid systems. Recently, significant breakthrough in improving selectivity of monoboronic acid to glucose has been reported by Jiang's group [6]. They developed a highly selective and sensitive ratiometric fluorescent chemosensor for glucose in aqueous solution based on an amphiphilic monoboronic acid receptor containing a pyrene fluorophore, which formed ordered aggregates and produced an excimer emission at the presence of glucose as one glucose molecule could bind with two boronic acid groups, resulting in the pyrene fluorophores being brought into closer proximity and enabling the pyrene excimer emission. However, it was not proved to be applicable for real sample analysis. When it is applied to glucose detection in biological samples, theoretically endogenous fluorophores or quenchers may severely interfere the determination accuracy [7]. Therefore, to develop selective and sensitive fluorescence methods based on monoboronic acid systems to afford reliable measurements of glucose in biological samples is still very significant and challenging.

Very recently, it was shown that boronic acid-functionalized microspheres could reduce endogenous interferences from biological matrix during analysis of 5-hydroxymethylcytosine and glycoproteins [16,17]. Herein, a sandwich boronate affinity sorbent assay (SBASA) for selective and ultrasensitive detection of serum glucose, inspired by the principle of enzyme-linked immuno sorbent assay, was developed for the first time based on the distinguished feature of glucose which can form a bidentate glucose–boronic complex. Glucose was bond onto a boronate affinity sorbent first, a boronic acid terminated fluorescent polymer was bond onto the glucose-bond sorbent after extensively washing, then the fluorescent polymer was desorbed after extensively washing the polymer bond sorbent, and its fluorescence was measured (Fig. 1 ). The fluorescent intensity of the desorbed polymer was proportional to the glucose concentration. In this strategy, it has two main merits to introduce the boronate affinity sorbent. On one hand, most interferences from the matrix could be eliminated after extensively washing the sorbent. On the other hand, glucose could be enriched on the sorbent if large volume sample is subjected, thus the sensitivity could be enhanced. In order to raise the fluorescent intensity, a boronic acid terminated fluorescent polymer with multiple repeated fluorogens, instead of a small organic molecule with a single fluorogen was utilized to amplify the fluorescent signal [18], producing a high sensitivity with a detection limit of 0.8μM. To our knowledge, it is the most sensitive boronate affinity based fluorescent detection system. It is also important that the method can well distinguish glucose from both its analogues and various potential interfering species in blood. Consequently, it has a great potential as a practical platform for blood glucose detection.

Phenylboronic acid (97%) was purchased from Alpha Aesar (Tianjin, China). Various amino acids and 9-anthracenemethanol were acquired from Sinopharm Group Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Boronate affinity sorbent was prepared in our laboratory by coupling 4-formylphenylboronic acid to aminated poly(glycidyl methacrylate) beads, the detailed preparation procedures will be reported elsewhere. Human serum was supplied from Northwest University Hospital. All the other chemical reagents were of analytical grade and provided by local suppliers. Ultrapure water (18.2MΩcm) was used throughout the experiments.

The fluorescence was recorded with a Hitachi F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured from a KBr window on a Bruker EOUINOX-55 FT-IR spectrophotometer. High-resolution mass spectrometry was measured with a Bruker micrOTOF-Q mass spectrometer. 1H NMR spectra were recorded on a Bruker AVANCEIII nuclear magnetic resonance spectrometer. Gel permeation chromatography (GPC) was run on Dionex UltiMate3000 at ambient temperature using THF as the eluent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of phenylboronic acid tagged ATRP initiator (3)
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis pathways of phenylboronic acid tagged ATRP initiator are shown in Fig. 2a. 0.5g of phenylboronic acid (1) was dissolved in 50mL anhydrous THF solution, and the mixture was stirred for 20min in ice water bath. Then 0.5mL (0.1mol) 2-bromoisobutyryl bromide (BIBB) and 0.5mL (0.1mol) triethylamine were added into the vessel slowly. The whole mixture was stirred for 3h in ice water bath, and was allowed to react for 12h at 35°C. Finally, the solvent was evaporated, the resulting residues were purified by silica column chromatography [petroleum ether (PE): ethyl acetate (EA)=1:2]. The product was characterized by high-resolution mass spectrometer, its ESI-MS spectra at negative mode shows a peak at m/z =286.0074 (Figure S1, Supporting information), it matched reasonably well with the theoretical value (286.0096, M−H).

The synthesis pathways of phenylboronic acid tagged ATRP initiator are shown in Fig. 2a. 0.5g of phenylboronic acid (1) was dissolved in 50mL anhydrous THF solution, and the mixture was stirred for 20min in ice water bath. Then 0.5mL (0.1mol) 2-bromoisobutyryl bromide (BIBB) and 0.5mL (0.1mol) triethylamine were added into the vessel slowly. The whole mixture was stirred for 3h in ice water bath, and was allowed to react for 12h at 35°C. Finally, the solvent was evaporated, the resulting residues were purified by silica column chromatography [petroleum ether (PE): ethyl acetate (EA)=1:2]. The product was characterized by high-resolution mass spectrometer, its ESI-MS spectra at negative mode shows a peak at m/z =286.0074 (Figure S1, Supporting information), it matched reasonably well with the theoretical value (286.0096, M−H).

The fluorescent monomer, 9-anthracenemethyl acrylate (9-AnMA, 4) was prepared by one step protocol as shown in Fig. 2b. 1.40g 9-anthracenemethanol (2) was added into a 100-mL round-bottom flask with 65mL anhydrous methylene chloride solution. After dissolution of 9-anthracenemethanol, 606mg triethylamine was added into the flask, the mixture was stirred in ice-water bath and bubbled with nitrogen atmosphere for 3h. Then 500μL acryloyl chloride (540mg) was added into the reaction system drop by drop. The mixture was stirred for one hour in ice-water bath, and was allowed to further react for 12h at room temperature. After the reaction completed, the solvent was evaporated, and the resulting residues were purified by silica column chromatography (PE:EA=5:1), the reaction yield was determined to be 70%. 1HNMR spectroscopy proved that the desired product was obtained (Supporting information Figure S2). The peaks with chemical shift (δ) at 8.42 (anth-H10), 8.31 (anth-H1 and -H8), 7.96 (anth-H4 and -H5), 7.59 (anth-H2 and -H7), and 7.41 (anth-H3 and -H6) were assigned for the anthracene ring. And the peak with chemical shift at 6.19 (s) was ascribed to methylene protons. The peak with chemical shift at 6.41, 6.08 and 5.87 were attributed to alkene protons. In addition, results from FT-IR also proved 9-AnMA was successfully produced (see Section 3).

The fluorescent monomer, 9-anthracenemethyl acrylate (9-AnMA, 4) was prepared by one step protocol as shown in Fig. 2b. 1.40g 9-anthracenemethanol (2) was added into a 100-mL round-bottom flask with 65mL anhydrous methylene chloride solution. After dissolution of 9-anthracenemethanol, 606mg triethylamine was added into the flask, the mixture was stirred in ice-water bath and bubbled with nitrogen atmosphere for 3h. Then 500μL acryloyl chloride (540mg) was added into the reaction system drop by drop. The mixture was stirred for one hour in ice-water bath, and was allowed to further react for 12h at room temperature. After the reaction completed, the solvent was evaporated, and the resulting residues were purified by silica column chromatography (PE:EA=5:1), the reaction yield was determined to be 70%. 1HNMR spectroscopy proved that the desired product was obtained (Supporting information Figure S2). The peaks with chemical shift (δ) at 8.42 (anth-H10), 8.31 (anth-H1 and -H8), 7.96 (anth-H4 and -H5), 7.59 (anth-H2 and -H7), and 7.41 (anth-H3 and -H6) were assigned for the anthracene ring. And the peak with chemical shift at 6.19 (s) was ascribed to methylene protons. The peak with chemical shift at 6.41, 6.08 and 5.87 were attributed to alkene protons. In addition, results from FT-IR also proved 9-AnMA was successfully produced (see Section 3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the boronic acid terminated poly (9-anthracenemethyl acrylate-co-methyl methacrylate) [(HO)2B-P(AnMA-co-MMA), 5]
ce:section.ce:para: 0.30g 4,4′-dinonyl-2,2′-bipyridine (1.90mmol) was added into a Schlenk tube with a stir bar. 0.01g phenylboronic acid tagged ATRP initiator (0.03mmol) and 0.46g 9-AnMA (1.74mmol) were dissolved in anisole (4.0g) containing methyl methacrylate (1.74g, 17.42mmol) in a round-bottom flask (10mL). After purging with Ar atmosphere for 30min, 0.14g CuBr (0.95mmol) was added to the above suspension. The flask was placed in a thermostatic oil bath at 90°C and the suspension was stirred for 11h under Ar atmosphere [19]. When the reaction completed, the mixture was then diluted with 20mL of THF and flowed through a short alumina column. The column was washed with 15mL THF for three times. The resulting colorless flowthrough was dripped into hexane (500mL) to precipitate the polymer. The precipitation was repeated three times. The obtained white precipitate was filtered by filter paper and dried under vacuum to provide white solid powder (0.28g). The fluorescent polymer was denoted as (HO)2B-P(AnMA-co-MMA). 1H-NMR (CDCl3): δ 8.49 (s, 1H, anth-H10), 8.31 (d, 2H, anth-H1,8), 8.04 (d, 2H, anth-H4,5), 7.58 (s, 2H, anth-H2,7), 7.46 (s, 2H, anth-H3,6), 6.05 (s, 2H, 9-anth-CH2O), methylene protons (s, 3.73–3.49 (multiplet)) and δ 1.94–1.72 (m, CH(CO)Br) (Supporting information Figure S3).

0.30g 4,4′-dinonyl-2,2′-bipyridine (1.90mmol) was added into a Schlenk tube with a stir bar. 0.01g phenylboronic acid tagged ATRP initiator (0.03mmol) and 0.46g 9-AnMA (1.74mmol) were dissolved in anisole (4.0g) containing methyl methacrylate (1.74g, 17.42mmol) in a round-bottom flask (10mL). After purging with Ar atmosphere for 30min, 0.14g CuBr (0.95mmol) was added to the above suspension. The flask was placed in a thermostatic oil bath at 90°C and the suspension was stirred for 11h under Ar atmosphere [19]. When the reaction completed, the mixture was then diluted with 20mL of THF and flowed through a short alumina column. The column was washed with 15mL THF for three times. The resulting colorless flowthrough was dripped into hexane (500mL) to precipitate the polymer. The precipitation was repeated three times. The obtained white precipitate was filtered by filter paper and dried under vacuum to provide white solid powder (0.28g). The fluorescent polymer was denoted as (HO)2B-P(AnMA-co-MMA). 1H-NMR (CDCl3): δ 8.49 (s, 1H, anth-H10), 8.31 (d, 2H, anth-H1,8), 8.04 (d, 2H, anth-H4,5), 7.58 (s, 2H, anth-H2,7), 7.46 (s, 2H, anth-H3,6), 6.05 (s, 2H, 9-anth-CH2O), methylene protons (s, 3.73–3.49 (multiplet)) and δ 1.94–1.72 (m, CH(CO)Br) (Supporting information Figure S3).

20.0mg of boronate affinity sorbent was added to 2.0mL glucose solution, the suspension was gentle shaken for 30min. After centrifugation the supernatant was discarded, and the sorbent was washed with 2mL 20mM NH3–NH4Cl buffer solution (pH 9) for three times. Then 2mL fluorescent polymer solution (2mM in THF, pH 9) was added to the sorbent, the mixture was incubated for 30min with gentle shake, and the sorbent was washed with 2mL THF (pH 9) for three times. After that, the sorbent was incubated with 3mL THF containing 1mM HCl for 30min to desorb the boronic acid terminated fluorescent polymer. Finally, the fluorescent spectra were recorded under excitation at 358nm, the slit width was set at 5nm and the thickness of colorimetric ware is 10mm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the boronic acid terminated fluorescent polymer
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, a boronic acid terminated fluorescent polymer with multiple repeated fluorogens was exploited to amplify the detection signal of glucose [18]. Atom transfer radical polymerization (ATRP) was exploited to synthesize the fluorescent polymer [20,21]. A phenylboronic acid tagged ATRP initiator was synthesized first and was used to initiate copolymerization of 9-anthracenemethyl acrylate (a fluorescent monomer) and methyl methacrylate (MMA) to produce the fluorescent polymer [(HO)2B-P(AnMA-co-MMA)]. In this case, a boronic acid group is directly incorporated in the end of the polymeric chain without postpolymerization strategies.

FT-IR was used to characterize the chemical structures of the fluorescent monomer and polymer, and the results are shown in Fig. 3A. Compared with the spectrum of 9-anthracenemethanol (curve a), there appeared a strong absorption at 1732cm−1 in the spectrum of 9-anthracenemethyl acrylate (curve b), which is ascribed to its ester carbonyl, and the absorption at 3432cm−1 decreased very significantly due to reaction of hydroxyl in 9-anthracenemethanol with acryloyl chloride (curve b). For the (HO)2B-P(AnMA-co-MMA) (curve c), the absorption at 2994 and 2950cm−1 is assigned to C–H stretching vibration of –CH3 in MMA. A broad absorbance between 3700 and 3100cm−1 is ascribed to N–H/O–H stretching vibrations. The peaks at 1343, 1281, 1187cm−1 are respectively attributed to B–O stretching vibration, C–N stretching vibration and B–O–H bending vibration in phenylboronic acid tagged ATRP initiator. These results indicated that the boronic acid terminated poly (9-anthracenemethyl acrylate-co-methyl methacrylate) [(HO)2B-P(AnMA-co-MMA)] was successfully produced. The polymer was also characterized by 1H-NMR (CDCl3) (Fig. S3). The peaks with chemical shift at 8.49–7.46 were assigned to the anthracene ring, the peak with chemical shift at 6.05 was ascribed to the 9-methylene group connected to the anthracene ring and the one at 3.58 were attributed to the PMMA protons. The results further proved the successful production of the polymer. Fluorescent spectra of the polymer were recorded, and its maximum fluorescent excitation and emission wavelengths were separately determined to be 358 and 418nm (Fig. 3B), which were consistent with the results reported in the literature [19].

FT-IR was used to characterize the chemical structures of the fluorescent monomer and polymer, and the results are shown in Fig. 3A. Compared with the spectrum of 9-anthracenemethanol (curve a), there appeared a strong absorption at 1732cm−1 in the spectrum of 9-anthracenemethyl acrylate (curve b), which is ascribed to its ester carbonyl, and the absorption at 3432cm−1 decreased very significantly due to reaction of hydroxyl in 9-anthracenemethanol with acryloyl chloride (curve b). For the (HO)2B-P(AnMA-co-MMA) (curve c), the absorption at 2994 and 2950cm−1 is assigned to C–H stretching vibration of –CH3 in MMA. A broad absorbance between 3700 and 3100cm−1 is ascribed to N–H/O–H stretching vibrations. The peaks at 1343, 1281, 1187cm−1 are respectively attributed to B–O stretching vibration, C–N stretching vibration and B–O–H bending vibration in phenylboronic acid tagged ATRP initiator. These results indicated that the boronic acid terminated poly (9-anthracenemethyl acrylate-co-methyl methacrylate) [(HO)2B-P(AnMA-co-MMA)] was successfully produced. The polymer was also characterized by 1H-NMR (CDCl3) (Fig. S3). The peaks with chemical shift at 8.49–7.46 were assigned to the anthracene ring, the peak with chemical shift at 6.05 was ascribed to the 9-methylene group connected to the anthracene ring and the one at 3.58 were attributed to the PMMA protons. The results further proved the successful production of the polymer. Fluorescent spectra of the polymer were recorded, and its maximum fluorescent excitation and emission wavelengths were separately determined to be 358 and 418nm (Fig. 3B), which were consistent with the results reported in the literature [19].

ce:section.ce:section-title: SBASA for glucose detection utilizing boronic acid-terminated fluorescent polymers
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that boronic acid can specifically recognize molecules with cis-diols via forming boronate ester in weak alkaline solutions. And boronic acid was broadly employed as a recognition unit for detection of glucose. Based on the unique feature that glucose has two binding sites to boronic acid [13], a SBASA for glucose detection using the above prepared boronic acid-terminated fluorescent polymers was established following the principle proposed in Fig. 1. In order to reach good assay performance, experimental conditions, such as adsorption time of glucose and the boronic acid terminated fluorescent polymer on the boronate affinity adsorbent, dose of the adsorbent were optimized to produce stronger fluorescent signal, yielding more sensitive detection of glucose. Effect of contacting time of glucose solution with the boronate affinity adsorbents on the fluorescent intensity was investigated in the range of 5–60min. The results showed that the fluorescent intensity increased sharply with the increasing adsorption time before 30min and remained constant afterwards (Fig. 4a). Therefore, 30min was selected as the optimal adsorption time. Similarly, effect of the adsorption time of the fluorescent polymer on the fluorescence intensities was also considered, and 30min was selected as the optimal adsorption time for the polymer (Fig. 4b). Finally, the influence of the dose of adsorbent on the fluorescence intensities was studied by varying the amount from 5 to 25mg. The maximum intensity was achieved at 20mg of the adsorbent (Fig. 4c). Hence, 20mg of the adsorbent was selected for subsequent experiments.

After optimizing the experimental conditions, the capability of the SBASA for glucose detection was investigated. Fig. 5A shows the fluorescent spectra of the eluted polymer with increasing glucose concentration from 0 to 5.0mM. The results indicated that with the glucose concentration enhanced, the fluorescence intensities increased gradually. The fluorescence intensity at 418nm was used to establish the analytical method, and it was found that there is a good linear relationship (R 2 =0.992) between the fluorescence intensities and logarithm of the glucose concentration [lgC(glucose)] in the concentration range from 0.003 to 3.0mM (Fig. 5B). Limit of detection (LOD) of this assay method was determined to be 0.8μM (3σ).

As a boronic acid-based glucose sensor, the interferences from other cis-diol compounds are big concerns. In order to examine the selectivity of the SBASA for glucose, its responses to other saccharides including fructose, lactose, mannitol, and sucrose were investigated (Fig. 6 ). Although the monoboronic acid group usually has good response to saccharides (fructose mainly) to form boronate ester, the fluorescence signal of these four sugars are negligible in the proposed method, which indicated that the SBASA can well recognize glucose from other saccharides. Additionally, in order to test if the SBASA method could be applied to detect serum glucose, we studied the effect of other potential interfering substances existing in serum, including various amino acids, dopamine, nucleotides, ascorbic acid and HSA. As shown in Fig. 6, the assay method has little response to these concerning substances, indicating very good selectivity to glucose. It should be pointed out that the real concentrations of the interfering substances are much lower than that of glucose in blood [22], so that the accuracy of our assay was guaranteed. HSA usually impact glucose detection when using fluorescent method, however, it is exciting to note that HSA had no obvious response in this proposed sandwich assay system, which allow to perform blood glucose detection without laborious and time-consuming HSA removal procedures [23–27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of glucose in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of the SBASA for glucose detection in real samples were investigated using human serum of a healthy individual as a representative sample. The human serum was diluted 40-fold by NH3–NH4Cl buffer solution (pH 9), and was then directly subjected to the assay. The glucose concentration of the sample was determined to be 8.0±0.1mM (n =3), which were in good agreement with the normal glucose levels in human serum (2.5–20mM). The analysis accuracy of the method was checked by recovery experiments. Three concentration levels of glucose (0.40, 4.0, and 40.0mM glucose) were added in the same sample as measured above. The results are presented in Table 1 . It was found that the recoveries were in the range of 90.5–100%, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were in the range of 1.2–3.8%. This indicates that this SBASA system is feasible for the accurate determination of glucose in real samples.

The SBASA was compared with other recently reported fluorescent methods for glucose detection based on boronic acid recognition (Table 2 ). It can be found that the proposed method is applicable to real sample analysis, and it avoids the laborious and time-consuming procedures for protein removal. In addition, the proposed method has a wider linear range and higher sensitivity for glucose detection. It should be noted that most fluorescent detection methods for glucose which based on boronic acid recognition, especially monoboronic acid recognition systems lack selectivity among cis-diols, therefore other monosaccharides and cis-diols can largely affect the accuracy of glucose determination [14,26–32]; And some theoretically endogenous fluorophores or quenchers would severely interfere the determination accuracy [7,23,24]. In the SBASA method, on one hand, the unique feature that one glucose molecule could bind with two boronic acid groups was used to enhance the selectivity; on the other hand, most interferences from the sample matrix could be removed with extensively washing the boronate affinity sorbent after capturing glucose molecules, which also improved the selectivity. As mentioned above, the boronic acid terminated fluorescent polymer was used to amplify the fluorescent signal, leading to higher sensitivity. Given the above advantages, we expect that the SBASA method will find its wide applicability, and this concept for glucose assay presented here can be developed into a clinical diagnostic tool.

In summary, a boronic acid terminated fluorescent polymer with multiple repeated fluorogens was synthesized by ATRP. Inspired by the principle of enzyme-linked immuno sorbent assay, a sandwich boronate affinity sorbent assay (SBASA) for selective and ultrasensitive detection of serum glucose was developed for the first time based on the unique feature of glucose which can form a bidentate glucose–boronic complex, using the fluorescent polymer for signal amplification. Compared with other recently reported fluorescent methods for glucose detection based on boronic acid recognition, the SBASA has higher selectivity and can avoid the laborious and time-consuming procedures for protein removal. In addition, the proposed method has a wider linear range and higher sensitivity for glucose detection. Moreover, the developed method was successfully applied to glucose detection in human serum samples, which verify the feasibility and reliability of glucose detection in complex environments. The simple, rapid, convenient, and reliable SBASA provided a new concept and an attractive idea for glucose monitoring.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation in China (Nos. 21575114 and 21475104); and the Scientific Research Program Funded by Shaanxi Provincial Education Department (No. 16JS114).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.03.093.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Disorders of glucose metabolism in acute stroke patients
Underwater adhesive of marine organisms as the vital link between biological science and material science
Use of mercaptophenylboronic acid functionalized gold nanoparticles in a sensitive and selective dynamic light scattering assay for glucose detection in serum
Enzymatic electrochemical glucose biosensors by mesoporous 1D hydroxyapatite-on-2D reduced graphene oxide
Glucose sensing via aggregation and the use of “Knock-Out” binding to improve selectivity
Boron-doped graphene quantum dots for selective glucose sensing based on the “Abnormal” aggregation-induced photoluminescence enhancement
Design strategies for water-soluble small molecular chromogenic and fluorogenic probes
Fluorescence turn-on detection of glucose via the Ag nanoparticle mediated release of a perylene probe

Immobilization of carbon dots in molecularly imprinted microgels for optical sensing of glucose at physiological pH
Selective sensing of saccharides using simple boronic acids and their aggregates
Fluorescent graphene quantum dots with a boronic acid appended bipyridinium salt to sense monosaccharides in aqueous solution
Selective fluorescence detection of monosaccharides using a material composite formed between graphene oxide and boronate-based receptors
Spectroscopic quantification of 5-hydroxymethylcytosine in genomic DNA using boric acid-functionalized nano-microsphere fluorescent probes
Detection of glycoprotein through fluorescent boronic acid-based molecularly imprinted polymer
Recent progress on polymer-based fluorescent and colorimetric chemosensors
Synthesis and application of fluorescently labeled phthalic anhydride (PA) functionalized polymers by ATRP
Copper-mediated living radical polymerization (atom transfer radical polymerization and copper(0) mediated polymerization): from fundamentals to bioapplications
Preparation of an internal surface reversed-phase restricted-access material for the analysis of hydrophobic molecules in biological matrices
Pattern recognition of monosaccharides via a virtual lectin array constructed by boronate affinity-based pH-featured encoding
Synthesis-modification integration: one-step fabrication of boronic acid functionalized carbon dots for fluorescent blood sugar sensing
Fluorescence enhancement of cadmium selenide quantum dots assembled on silver nanoparticles and its application to glucose detection

A boronate-linked linear-hyperbranched polymeric nanovehicle for pH-dependent tumor-targeted drug delivery
Boronate affinity fluorescent nanoparticles for förster resonance energy transfer inhibition assay of cis-diol biomolecules
Fluorescent quantum dots with boronic acid substituted viologens to sense glucose in aqueous solution
Competitive analysis of saccharides or dopamine by boronic acid-functionalized CdSe–ZnS quantum dots
Optical detection of glucose by CdS quantum dots immobilized in smart microgels
A polymer-based ratiometric intracellular glucose sensor

Glucose-mediated assembly of phenylboronic acid modified CdTe/ZnTe/ZnS quantum dots for intracellular glucose probing

Ratiometric fluorescent probe based on gold nanoclusters and alizarin red-boronic acid for monitoring glucose in brain microdialysate
Glucose sensing via polyanion formation and induced pyrene excimer emission
Surface modification and ratiometric fluorescence dual function enhancement for visual and fluorescent detection of glucose based on dual-emission quantum dots hybrid

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Chaozhan Wang is Professor of analytical chemistry at the College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Northwest University, China. His research interests focus on the preparation of adsorption and separation materials, analysis of complex samples, sample pretreatment, protein purification, and protein interactions.
Yinli Li is currently working toward the master degree at the College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Northwest University, China. Her research interests focus on bioanalysis.

Yinmao Wei is Head of the Separation Science Team at the Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Natural Function Molecule Chemistry of Ministry of Education, and Director of Analytical Chemistry Discipline in College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Northwest University, China. He has been working for nearly 20 years on the development of separation materials, chromatographic theory, and sample pre-treatment methods.
